elements.sent-2411-002: This article proposes a Variational Quantum Algorithm (VQA) to solve linear and nonlinear thermofluid dynamic transport equations.
elements.sent-2411-003: The hybrid classical-quantum framework is applied to problems governed by the heat, wave, and Burgers' equation in combination with different engineering boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-004: Topics covered include the consideration of non-constant material properties and upwind-biased first- and higher-order approximations, widely used in engineering Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), by the use of a mask function.
elements.sent-2411-005: The framework is able to convert band matrices arising from Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) discretized on structured grids into quantum gates, thus contributing to the development of a modular library for quantum computing translations of CFD procedures.
elements.sent-2411-006: Verification examples demonstrate high predictive agreement with classical methods.
elements.sent-2411-007: Furthermore, the scalability analysis shows a polylog complexity in the number of qubits of the quantum circuits involved.
elements.sent-2411-008: Remaining challenges refer to the implicit construction of upwind schemes.
elements.sent-2411-009: Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) has been established as a central discipline in computational science and engineering with applications ranging from climate research, to energy conversion or transportation and biomedical industries.
elements.sent-2411-010: The interest in numerical resolution of larger spatial and temporal scales encounters extremely expensive and energy-intensive simulations on classical hardware \cite{Nasa2030 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In this respect, Quantum Computers (QCs) promise a computational potential that cannot be achieved by classical hardware \cite{Jaksch2023a .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The research field of Quantum CFD (QCFD) is rapidly growing and can be divided into quantum-inspired approaches \cite{Gourianov2022,Kiffner2023,Peddinti2024 , that are not necessarily implemented on quantum hardware, and methods that are closely oriented to the capabilities and restrictions of quantum hardware \cite{Steijl2018,Cao2013, Harrow2009,Gaitan2020,Oz2021,Kacewicz2006,Chen2021,Brearley2024,Suau2021,Preskill2018,Kyriienko2021,Demirdjian2022,BravoPrieto2023,Sato2021,Leong2022,Leong2023,Leong2024,Over2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This work is focused on the second type of methods and their application to CFD.
elements.sent-2411-014: Steijl and Barakos~\cite{Steijl2018 used a hybrid classical-quantum approach for solving the Poisson problem in the vortex-in-cell method via a quantum Fourier algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-015: A similar strategy is employed in the work of Cao~et~al.~\cite{Cao2013 to approximate the Poisson equation in the predictor-corrector solver of the incompressible Navier-Stokes equation.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Here, the Quantum Linear Solver of Equations (QLSE) proposed by Harrow, Hassadim, and Lloyd~(HHL)~\cite{Harrow2009 is applied.}
elements.sent-2411-017: In Ref.~\cite{Gaitan2020 and \cite{Oz2021 , the Quantum Amplitude Estimation Algorithm (QAEA), described by Kacewicz~\cite{Kacewicz2006 , solves the system of coupled Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs), following the spatial discretization of the Navier-Stokes and the Burgers' equation, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The incremental time application of the HHL method is employed in Ref.~\cite{Chen2021 to approximate compressible flows after discretizing the Navier-Stokes equation with classical finite volumes.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Another methodology employs the Hamiltonian simulation for transport problems as it has been proposed by Brearley~et.~al~\cite{Brearley2024 and Over~et.~al~\cite{Over2024b for the advection and the advection-diffusion equation, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Instead of the QLSE approach, the system dynamics of the discrete problems are herein unitarized for a time evolution strategy, which is suitable for QCs.
elements.sent-2411-021: Unfortunately, these algorithms have to devote a range of qubits to error corrections, which significantly increases the total qubit count.
elements.sent-2411-022: Therefore, these algorithms are more appropriate for future fault-tolerant QC than for the current Noise Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) era \cite{Suau2021,Preskill2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs) provide an alternative for solving CFD problems on NISQ devices.
elements.sent-2411-024: VQA procedures rely on the combination of classical and quantum hardware, and require reformulating the governing Partial Differential Equation (PDE) as an optimization problem.
elements.sent-2411-025: The QC part of the employed hardware is dedicated to efficiently evaluating the cost function, while the classical hardware drives the optimizer for the parameterized solution.
elements.sent-2411-026: VQAs employ shallow circuits with a small number of gate operations, thereby reducing potential decoherence errors.
elements.sent-2411-027: Recent applications of VQAs on fluid dynamics are reported in Refs.~\cite{Demirdjian2022,BravoPrieto2023,Kyriienko2021,Sato2021,Leong2022,Leong2023,Leong2024,Over2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Some of these approaches are inspired by Physics Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), where quantum neural networks are trained variationally \cite{Kyriienko2021 to solve the Navier-Stokes equation for supersonic nozzle flows.}
elements.sent-2411-029: In contrast, the work of Demirdjian~et~al.~\cite{Demirdjian2022 separates the discretized Burgers’ advection-diffusion equation in a finite series of unitary operations that are approximated with the variational quantum linear solver introduced in Ref.~\cite{BravoPrieto2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: In Sato~et~al.~\cite{Sato2021 , the potential energy minimization of the elliptic Poisson equation is treated with a VQA that linearly decomposes the equation's dynamics in a series of parameterized quantum circuits.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The energy minimization approach of Sato~et~al.~\cite{Sato2021 is adapted to time-dependent problems in Leong~et~al.~\cite{Leong2022,Leong2023,Leong2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The work of Over~et~al.~\cite{Over2024 extends the Hadamard test-based VQA, described in Lubasch~et~al.~\cite{Lubasch2020 , to the treatment of \text{PDE s in combination with a variety of engineering boundary conditions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The present investigation extends the scope of Ref.~\cite{Over2024 to more general nonlinear convection-diffusion-reaction problems in combination with a variety of engineering boundary conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-034: To this end, different approximations of convective kinematics frequently used in engineering CFD, such as Central-Differencing-Scheme (CDS), Upwind-Differencing-Scheme (UDS) or the higher-order Linear-Upwind-Differencing-Scheme (LUDS), and Quadratic-Upstream-Interpolation-for-Convective-Kinematics (QUICK), are integrated into the VQA framework, additionally the occurrence of inhomogeneous material properties is also considered.
elements.sent-2411-035: The aforementioned studies are based on a general framework of encoding arbitrary band matrices in the Hadamard-based VQA \cite{Lubasch2020 , maintaining an efficient implementation with optimal gate complexity in the number of qubits.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The publication is structured as follows: Section~\ref{sec:Math describes the mathematical model and outlines the reformulation of the PDE into a minimization problem.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The general layout of the VQA and its major building blocks are explained in Sec.~\ref{sec:Quantum , while Sec.~\ref{sec:QCFD provides details of the quantum circuits representing the upwind-biased convective kinematics as well as the inhomogeneous material properties including the related complexity analysis.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: VQA-results obtained for the wave, heat, and Burgers' equations are compared with classical approaches in Sec.~\ref{sec:Results and Sec.~\ref{sec:Conclusion is devoted to conclusions.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The quantum circuits presented in this study have been emulated with IBM's Qiskit environment v.0.42.1~\cite{Qiskit , following a little-endian convention in the binary representation.}

elements.sent-2411-574: In this two-step strategy, the global search of the gradient-free initial optimizer provides a trained starting point for the subsequent gradient-based local optimization.
elements.sent-2411-575: The preceding global optimization may avoid potential problems of local optimizers due to vanishing gradients, also called Barren Plateaus (BPs).
elements.sent-2411-576: The global optimizer is initialized with approximately $100$ samples randomly distributed over the optimization manifold.
elements.sent-2411-577: These samples are updated for $10$ iterations without considering gradient information and the most optimal set is used to initiate the local optimization.
elements.sent-2411-578: For subsequent time steps, the optimal parameters ($\upb^{l-1}$) of the previous time step serve as initialization for successive restarts, and the PSO step is skipped.
elements.sent-2411-579: The gradient-based (local) optimization employs a version of the well-established BFGS approach~\cite{Nocedal2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-580: The convergence of the gradient-based optimizer is judged by the l$_2$-norm of the derivative concerning a defined tolerance, i.e., $tol \, > \|\nabla J(\upb^l)\|_{\text{l _2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-581: The derivative of the cost function is approximated by the parameter-shift rule \cite{Schuld2019,Banchi2021,Crooks2019 .}

elements.sent-2411-713: The method combines FD approximations for differential operators with an optimization problem that minimizes the weak residual formulation of the associated governing PDE.
elements.sent-2411-714: The approach implicitly treats symmetric discretization contributions, while non-symmetric/biased discretizations, e.g., to approximate convective kinematics, are explicitly treated.
elements.sent-2411-715: It supports non-homogeneous diffusivity and the encoding of classical, upwind-biased schemes of first (UDS) and higher-order (LUDS, QUICK) into the quantum concept, which, in contrast to central differences, do not generate dispersion waves even at high Reynolds/P\'{eclet numbers.}
elements.sent-2411-716: The VQA results generally seem to be in very good agreement with those obtained using classical methods.
elements.sent-2411-717: The remarkable agreement covers a wide range of benchmark cases and transport phenomena and include different numerical approaches, for example ansatz depths, discretization schemes and boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-718: This demonstrates that the proposed VQA method can solve various types of transport PDEs.
elements.sent-2411-719: The agreement is particularly excellent for four qubits, while some deviations were observed for six qubits, consistent with the existing literature \cite{Sato2021,Leong2022,Over2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-720: The deviations are attributed to the ansatz functions and could be addressed using an appropriate regularization of the objective functional and other strategies that help to improve the convergence of the optimization \cite{Sack22,Friedrich22,Yao25,Barthel25 .}
elements.sent-2411-721: Moreover, it is demonstrated that a physically appropriate modeling, e.g., by using upwind-biased schemes for convective kinematics, reduces the optimization costs of the hybrid VQA approach.
elements.sent-2411-722: In particular, the use of upwind differencing methods achieves the lowest cost function contributions, the lowest ansatz depth, and the lowest optimization effort, which can be combined with a higher spatial resolution to solve accuracy problems.
elements.sent-2411-723: Open challenges for amplitude phase estimation in terms of $\min$/$\max$~operations and strategies to regularize the ansatz to suppress long-range correlations will also be addressed.
